metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact heading string (or a stable substring anchor) that `test_release_workflow_pins.py` should `grep -q` for in `docs/releasing.md` — e.g. `grep -q 'full-SHA action pins' docs/releasing.md` or the literal section title `## Pinning`.
findings.finding: The docs/releasing.md AC specifies 'a grep for the SHA-pin rationale heading' as one of the three required substrings verified by `test_release_workflow_pins.py`, but does not state the exact string/heading that the grep must match. The test implementer must invent the heading text, and there is no guarantee the docs author uses the same string the test expects.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — sixth bullet (docs/releasing.md), specifically item (a)/(c) of the three-grep assertion
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a grep for the SHA-pin rationale heading' with the concrete command: `grep -q '## Pinning' docs/releasing.md` (or whichever heading the plan mandates for the section), consistent with the plan's instruction that the section is named 'Pinning'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that exercises the `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt && python -m build --no-isolation` path end-to-end — e.g. a `make test-release-build` target that installs from the lock file into a temp venv and runs `python -m build --no-isolation` against a minimal stub, or a nightly/dry-run release job — so the lock file is validated before a real release trigger.
findings.finding: The cutover to `--no-isolation` + hash-locked build environment is proven only by a static text-presence check (`test_release_workflow_pins.py` asserting the string `--no-isolation` appears in `release.yml`). No AC or step exercises the actual `pip install --require-hashes … && python -m build --no-isolation` code path end-to-end, so the lock file could be incomplete, the hashes could be wrong, or the build could fail in ways that are invisible pre-merge.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet (hash-locked build tools) and fourth bullet (--no-isolation enforcement)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Makefile target `test-release-build` that: (1) creates a temp venv, (2) runs `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`, (3) runs `python -m build --no-isolation --outdir /tmp/test-dist`, and (4) asserts the expected wheel and sdist appear. Add an AC: 'Running `make test-release-build` succeeds — the lock file is complete, hashes are valid, and `--no-isolation` build produces a wheel and sdist.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an assertion in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` (or a dedicated fixture test) that reads `.github/release-requirements.txt` and confirms it contains at least one `--hash=sha256:` entry, so a hash-free requirements file is caught by `make test` pre-merge.
findings.finding: The test suite verifies that the workflow YAML invokes `pip install --require-hashes` (static text check) but no test asserts that `.github/release-requirements.txt` itself actually contains `--hash=sha256:` entries. A developer could create a plain, hash-free requirements file; `test_release_workflow_pins.py` would still pass, but `pip install --require-hashes` would fail at real release time — a silent gap between the green test suite and a broken release.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet; Scope — `.github/release-requirements.txt`; `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py`
findings.suggested_fix:
In `test_release_workflow_pins.py`, add a test such as:
```python
def test_release_requirements_contains_hashes():
    req = pathlib.Path('.github/release-requirements.txt').read_text()
    assert '--hash=sha256:' in req, (
        '.github/release-requirements.txt must be generated with '
        'pip-compile --generate-hashes; no hash entries found'
    )
```
This ensures the file was actually generated with `--generate-hashes` and that `--require-hashes` will not fail at release time due to a missing-hash file.

findings.finding: The plan uses a pipe-separated grep pattern to assert fragment-name presence (`evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp|evidence-artifacts`) but never specifies per-name cardinality. A single line in release.yml that concatenates all four names (e.g. in a comment or a single `echo`) would satisfy the grep while the actual upload-artifact steps remain absent — a substring false-positive per VCDT defect #1/#5.
findings.location: Scope section '.github/workflows/release-lint.yml' check (f); AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the pipe-or grep with four separate anchored greps: `grep -E "name:\s*evidence-shapin" .github/workflows/release.yml`, repeated for each fragment name. This ensures the names appear in the correct structural position.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the aggregation job explicitly in the plan (e.g. `release_evidence`, `needs: [build, mcp_verify, mcp_registry]`, no `id-token: write`), specifying its `needs:` list and permission level so the executor has no design choice to make.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'final aggregation step' that downloads all evidence fragments and concatenates them into `release-evidence.txt`, but does not name which job hosts this step or what its `needs:` dependencies and permissions should be. An executor must invent this design choice without guidance.
findings.location: What section — 'Print and retain release evidence' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet (release evidence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'A new `release_evidence` job (`needs: [build_dist, mcp_verify, mcp_registry]`, no elevated permissions) runs `actions/download-artifact` for each fragment and concatenates them: `cat evidence-shapin/tool-versions.txt evidence-build/build-info.txt evidence-mcp/mcp-info.txt > release-evidence.txt`, then uploads the consolidated artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pip-tools` to the `[dev]` extra in `pyproject.toml` (or document an explicit `pipx install pip-tools` bootstrap step in the plan and `docs/releasing.md`) so maintainers have a supported, in-repo path to run `pip-compile --generate-hashes` when regenerating `.github/release-requirements.txt`.
findings.finding: The plan names `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) as the canonical lock-file regeneration command in the Scope section, AC3, and the docs AC grep check, but `pip-tools` is not listed in `pyproject.toml`'s `[dev]` extra (verified by reading pyproject.toml lines 147-181) and is not resolvable in the installed environment. A developer following the documented procedure to regenerate `.github/release-requirements.txt` would silently lack the tool unless it is separately bootstrapped — a gap the plan never addresses.
findings.location: Scope section (.github/release-requirements.txt), AC3, docs AC; pyproject.toml [dev] extra
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"pip-tools"` to the `dev` extra in `pyproject.toml` alongside the other dev tooling, and add a `make lock-release` (or similar) target to `Makefile` that invokes `pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `pip-tools` (or `uv`) is provisioned for lock-file regeneration — either add `pip-tools` to a dev extra in `pyproject.toml`, document a `pipx install pip-tools` one-liner in the 'Pinning' section of `docs/releasing.md`, or add a CI step that installs it before running `pip-compile` — so the regeneration command documented in the AC is actually executable.
findings.finding: The plan mandates `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) as the lock-file generation tool — both for the initial checked-in `.github/release-requirements.txt` and for future pin updates — but never specifies how pip-tools is provisioned. It is absent from pyproject.toml dev extras, not present in the CI environment, and not mentioned in any existing workflow. Without a stated installation path (e.g., `pipx install pip-tools`, a dev-extra entry, or a CI step), a developer or CI job running the documented regeneration command (`pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file=.github/release-requirements.txt .github/release-requirements.in`) will fail with 'command not found'. The docs AC grep check (`grep -q 'pip-compile --generate-hashes' docs/releasing.md`) will pass even if the tool is unavailable.
findings.location: What section (pip-tools paragraph); Scope (.github/release-requirements.txt bullet); AC3; docs AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pip-tools` to the `[dev]` extra in `pyproject.toml` (e.g., `'pip-tools'`) so `make install` provisions it automatically, and note the version in the docs 'Pinning' section. Alternatively, document `pipx install pip-tools` as a one-time prerequisite alongside the `git-cliff==2.13.1` pattern already in docs/releasing.md.

findings.finding: The plan designates `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) as the canonical lock-file generation command and hard-codes `grep -q 'pip-compile --generate-hashes' docs/releasing.md` as a CI assertion, but pip-tools is not installed anywhere in the repo (not in `pyproject.toml` dev dependencies, no CI workflow references it). The plan does not specify how pip-tools itself is installed for the lock-regeneration step — creating a bootstrap gap: a developer who wants to regenerate `.github/release-requirements.txt` must separately discover and install pip-tools. If `pip-compile` is not available in the CI environment that runs `release-lint.yml`, the static assertion (`grep` check) would pass even though the docs command is not executable in that environment. Evidence class: Partially-verified (pip-compile --generate-hashes flags are real, but the installation precondition in CI/developer workflow is unspecified).
findings.location: Scope / `.github/release-requirements.txt` bullet / AC3 / docs AC grep check
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pip-tools` as a dev dependency in `pyproject.toml` (e.g. `pip-tools>=7` under `[project.optional-dependencies] dev`) so it is installed in the standard dev setup. Document the exact version in `docs/releasing.md`. Alternatively, provide a one-liner `pip install pip-tools==<pinned-version>` in the 'Pinning' section docs, explicitly noting this is the only tool that bootstraps outside the hash-locked env.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `persist-credentials: false` exception ambiguity: either (a) remove the 'unless a later git operation…' qualifier from the What section if no current release checkout needs persisted credentials, or (b) add an explicit exception mechanism to the AC and test (e.g., require an inline `# persist-credentials: true — needed for <reason>` comment that the test allowlists).
findings.finding: The 'What' section permits an exception to `persist-credentials: false` ('unless a later git operation demonstrably needs the persisted credential'), but the Acceptance Criteria and the described test (`test_release_workflow_pins.py`) enforce the setting unconditionally on every checkout with no exception path — an implementer who legitimately omits the flag per the 'What' clause will fail CI with no recourse described.
findings.location: What section ('Set `persist-credentials: false`…unless…'), AC #2 ('Every release checkout sets `persist-credentials: false`'), Scope (test description)
findings.suggested_fix: If no current release checkout actually requires persisted credentials (most release workflows don't), remove the 'unless…' qualifier from the What section so it matches the AC. If a future need arises, introduce it as a named exception via a review process documented in docs/releasing.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the pip-tools vs. uv pip compile equivalence: either (a) remove the `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` alternative and commit to pip-tools only (aligning the What section with the docs AC grep), or (b) verify that `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` produces pip `--require-hashes`-compatible output and update the docs AC grep to accept both spellings.
findings.finding: The plan presents `pip-compile --generate-hashes` (pip-tools) and `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as interchangeable alternatives in the What section ('pip-compile --generate-hashes (pip-tools) (or uv pip compile --generate-hashes)'), but then the docs AC hard-codes a grep for the pip-tools spelling: `grep -q 'pip-compile --generate-hashes' docs/releasing.md`. If an implementer chooses the uv variant the docs AC grep silently fails, breaking an equivalence the plan itself asserts. This is an unverified capability-equivalence assertion (T1c): the plan never confirms that both tools produce identically-structured output that pip's --require-hashes can consume, nor that uv pip compile --generate-hashes is a real flag.
findings.location: What section ('pip-compile --generate-hashes (pip-tools) (or uv pip compile --generate-hashes)'); AC3 docs grep; AC7 docs grep check
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the parenthetical `(or uv pip compile --generate-hashes)` from the What section to match the docs AC grep, or change the docs AC grep to `grep -qE '(pip-compile|uv pip compile) --generate-hashes' docs/releasing.md` and add a note that both tools produce compatible output.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the aggregation step either (a) uses `continue-on-error: true` (or `actions/download-artifact` with `pattern:` and `merge-multiple: true`) for the `evidence-artifacts` fragment so its absence is gracefully tolerated, OR (b) the aggregation job is only wired up in Finding 3's story — and document which approach is chosen so consumers of `release-evidence.txt` know it is incomplete until Finding 3 lands.
findings.finding: The evidence-aggregation step — which downloads all fragment artifacts and concatenates them into `release-evidence.txt` — is specified in this story, but `evidence-artifacts` (the wheel/sdist digest fragment from Finding 3) does not exist until Finding 3 lands. The plan does not specify whether the aggregation step tolerates the absence of `evidence-artifacts`, meaning the aggregation job may fail or silently produce an incomplete `release-evidence.txt` during the window between this story merging and Finding 3 merging.
findings.location: Scope section — evidence aggregation; AC bullet 6 (`evidence-shapin`/`evidence-build`/`evidence-mcp` consolidation); parenthetical `(evidence-artifacts is asserted by Finding 3's story after it lands)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--connect-timeout 30 --max-time 120` (or equivalent) to the `curl` download step in `mcp_verify` so a hung upstream fails fast with a clear non-zero exit, and document the timeout rationale alongside the pin.
findings.finding: The `curl` download step in `mcp_verify` specifies no connection timeout or max-time flag. If the upstream release server accepts the TCP connection but stalls on data transfer, the job will hang silently until GitHub Actions' 6-hour job timeout, blocking the entire release pipeline with no operator signal and no retry.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher steps; AC bullet 5 (`curl -fsSL "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the download step to: `curl -fsSL --connect-timeout 30 --max-time 120 "<exact-version-url>" -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step in `mcp_registry` that recomputes `sha256sum mcp-publisher` and checks it against a digest recorded as an output/annotation from `mcp_verify` (e.g., via `echo "mcp_binary_sha256=<hash>" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT` in mcp_verify and `grep -q "<hash>  mcp-publisher" <<<"$(sha256sum mcp-publisher)"` in mcp_registry), so the binary actually executed under `id-token: write` has a verified digest.
findings.finding: The mcp_registry job executes the mcp-publisher binary after downloading it from GitHub Actions artifact storage, but never re-verifies the binary's digest before execution. The sha256sum -c check in mcp_verify runs against the .tar.gz *archive*; the extracted binary is uploaded as an artifact and downloaded by mcp_registry — a separate storage round-trip during which the binary could be substituted. The plan's security contract ('the OIDC-privileged job never downloads or holds an unverified binary') is not achieved by the specified mechanism: the binary in the artifact *is* unverified at the point mcp_registry executes it.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; AC5 (mcp_verify/mcp_registry); Scope: .github/workflows/release.yml mcp_verify/mcp_registry split
findings.suggested_fix: In mcp_verify, after extracting the binary, add: `echo "mcp_binary_sha256=$(sha256sum mcp-publisher | awk '{print $1}')" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT`. In mcp_registry, after download-artifact, add: `echo "${{ needs.mcp_verify.outputs.mcp_binary_sha256 }}  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict`. This closes the artifact-storage trust gap and makes the security contract accurate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan (and enforce via a CI check or lock-file validation step) that `.github/release-requirements.txt` must be generated from a clean/isolated environment so that all transitive dependencies are included, and add a note that `--no-deps` is safe ONLY when the lock file is exhaustively complete — consider adding a `pip check` or `python -c 'import build, hatchling, twine'` step after the hash-locked install to catch missing transitive deps before the build.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt` as the hash-locked install mechanism, but does not state that the lock file generated by `pip-compile --generate-hashes` must exhaustively enumerate ALL transitive dependencies. With `--no-deps`, pip will not install any unlisted transitive dep — and will not error on a missing one either. If a transitive dep of build/hatchling/twine is absent from the lock file, the build will silently use whatever version happens to be pre-installed in the GitHub runner environment, defeating the hash-locking for that dep. The plan says the lock covers 'their transitive deps' but this is stated as a description, not as a hard requirement with an enforcement mechanism.
findings.location: What section: '--no-isolation' bullet; AC3: 'pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt'; Scope: .github/release-requirements.txt
findings.suggested_fix: Add a post-install sanity step: `python -c 'import build; import hatchling; import twine'` (and any other top-level imports needed) immediately after `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`. This converts the silent missing-dep failure mode into an immediate, visible import error. Also add to the plan: 'pip-compile must be run from a clean virtual environment (not from the dev env) to ensure all transitive deps are captured.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit, minimal `permissions:` block at the job level for every release job in `release.yml` (e.g., `mcp_registry: permissions: { id-token: write }` only; other jobs scoped to `contents: read` or similar), and extend `test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (e) to assert that `mcp_registry` declares no permissions beyond `id-token: write`.
findings.finding: The plan isolates `id-token: write` to the `mcp_registry` job but never specifies that `mcp_registry` (or other release jobs) declare an explicit, minimal `permissions:` block at the job level. Without a job-level `permissions:` declaration that drops all grants except the ones actually needed (e.g., `id-token: write` only for `mcp_registry`; possibly `contents: read` where artifacts are fetched), the job silently inherits the repository's or workflow's default permission level — which for many repositories includes `contents: write`, `packages: write`, and others. This means a compromised step inside `mcp_registry` (e.g., a malicious `mcp-publisher` binary that somehow passes the hash check in a future rotation error) would have far broader access than the OIDC token alone.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; Scope section — `release.yml`; AC line: 'mcp_registry (needs: mcp_verify; holds id-token: write)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `permissions:` key to each release job in `release.yml`. For `mcp_registry`: `permissions: { id-token: write }`. For `mcp_verify` and build jobs that only read/upload artifacts: `permissions: { contents: read }` (adjust if `actions/upload-artifact` or `download-artifact` requires `actions: write`). Set a workflow-level `permissions: {}` (deny-all default) so each job must explicitly opt in. Extend the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` job-permission assertion to verify `mcp_registry`'s `permissions` block contains only `id-token: write` and that no other job holds `id-token: write`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out note for the `--no-isolation` build change: document (in Scope or docs/releasing.md) the procedure to temporarily revert to an isolated build (e.g., removing `--no-isolation` and the `--require-hashes` install step) if the lock file is found to be incomplete, and note that `pip-compile --no-deps` requires all transitive deps to be explicitly listed in `.github/release-requirements.in`.
findings.finding: The switch from an isolated build (`python -m build`, default) to `--no-isolation` is described as an explicit, deliberate choice, but no rollback path is described if the hash-locked env in `.github/release-requirements.txt` is incomplete or mis-compiled — a situation that would silently fail the release build with no recovery procedure stated.
findings.location: What section ('This story uses `--no-isolation`'), Scope ('.github/release-requirements.txt (new)'), AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope entry for release-requirements.txt, add: 'If the locked env is incomplete, the emergency back-out is to revert the `--no-isolation` flag and the pre-install step in release.yml (restoring isolated-build behavior) via a fast-path Gerrit change; document this in docs/releasing.md under the Pinning section.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the evidence-fragment naming convention and dual-update rule (workflow aggregation step + `test_release_workflow_pins.py` check (f)) in `docs/releasing.md` or `CLAUDE.md` so future release stories have an explicit, findable contract to follow.
findings.finding: The evidence-fragment aggregation pattern (evidence-shapin / evidence-build / evidence-mcp + a consolidation job) is introduced as a new cross-job convention, but the extension contract — that any future story adding a fragment must update BOTH the workflow aggregation step AND the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` static assertion — is not captured in any design doc, CLAUDE.md, or ADR. Finding 3's story is told it will add `evidence-artifacts`, but the rule is implicit rather than codified, creating a silent coupling risk for all subsequent release stories.
findings.location: Scope section (`tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py`); AC bullet 6 (evidence fragment wiring); 'What' section (evidence fragment pattern)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection in `docs/releasing.md` 'Pinning' (or a new 'Release evidence' section) listing the fragment naming scheme (`evidence-<story-slug>`), the aggregation job structure, and the rule that any new fragment requires updating both the workflow's consolidation step and the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` fragment-name assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale to the plan for why bespoke Python static YAML parsing in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` is preferred over adopting `zizmor` (or `actionlint`) for the SHA-pin and persist-credentials checks — e.g., if `make test` integration, scoping to `release.yml` only, or avoiding a new binary dependency are the deciding factors, state them explicitly so the implementer understands the deliberate trade-off.
findings.finding: The plan chooses bespoke Python static YAML parsing (test_release_workflow_pins.py) to enforce SHA-pin and persist-credentials constraints in release.yml, but gives no positive rationale for this choice over established, purpose-built GitHub Actions security linters (e.g., zizmor) that natively understand action reference semantics, OIDC permission scopes, and credential-persistence properties. The plan states this is 'the single home for the static release checks' and 'run by make test' — the latter is a good codebase-alignment reason (make test is already gated) — but does not articulate why a bespoke parser is preferred, or why zizmor/actionlint was not adopted. A clearly-superior alternative exists for the SHA-pin and persist-credentials checks specifically: zizmor's `unpinned-uses` and `dangerous-write` rules would catch these with zero custom parsing code and would generalize to all workflows, not just release.yml.
findings.location: Scope: tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py; AC1 ('verified by a new, self-contained SHA-pin lint'); AC2 ('verified by test_release_workflow_pins.py'); What section: 'This story adds a new, self-contained SHA-pin lint'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate zizmor (and/or actionlint) as prior art for the SHA-pin and persist-credentials enforcement before committing to the bespoke `test_release_workflow_pins.py` grep approach — document in the plan why a purpose-built linter is insufficient or rejected in favor of the custom test.
findings.finding: The plan builds a bespoke YAML-parsing/grep test (test_release_workflow_pins.py) to enforce full-SHA uses:, persist-credentials: false, and --no-isolation in release.yml without considering or rejecting established prior art: zizmor (a dedicated, actively-maintained GitHub Actions security linter) already detects mutable action references and missing persist-credentials out of the box, and actionlint covers structural YAML correctness. The plan re-implements a subset of these tools' capabilities in a single-repo, grep-based form with no justification for the novel approach over the established alternatives.
findings.location: Scope section (tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py); AC1 ('verified by a new, self-contained workflow-lint/grep check'); AC2 ('verified by test_release_workflow_pins.py')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan section: 'Prior-art considered: zizmor (woodruffw/zizmor) detects mutable action pins and missing persist-credentials natively. We choose [zizmor / bespoke test] because [reason — e.g., zizmor is not yet pinned in this repo's CI and adding it is out of scope; the bespoke test also enforces --no-isolation which zizmor does not cover].'

findings.finding: AC6 names four artifact fragments in the release-lint.yml check (f) — `evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp|evidence-artifacts` — but the AC text and the scope section state that `evidence-artifacts` (wheel/sdist digests) is out of scope for this story and will be added by Finding 3. If the lint check asserts all four names in release.yml and this story doesn't add `evidence-artifacts`, the lint check will fail in PR CI, making the end-state unreachable within this story's scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6; Scope section 'release-lint.yml'; What section 'Print and retain release evidence'
findings.suggested_fix: In the release-lint.yml check (f), assert only the three fragment names this story delivers: `evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp`. Add a TODO comment noting that `evidence-artifacts` is wired by Finding 3. Update AC6 text to match: 'the three fragment names this story delivers … appear in release.yml; the consolidation step is present.'

findings.finding: The new `release-lint.yml` workflow is PR-triggered (on changes to `release.yml` / `docs/releasing.md`), but Gerrit pre-merge changes dispatch `gerrit-verify.yaml` via `workflow_dispatch` — GitHub PR triggers never fire for Gerrit-landed changes. This means AC1/AC2/AC4/AC5/AC6 enforcement (SHA-pin lint, persist-credentials, --no-isolation, permission structure, evidence wiring) is entirely bypassed in the Gerrit gate, and any regression to `release.yml` introduced by a Gerrit change would not be caught pre-merge.
findings.location: Scope section / `.github/workflows/release-lint.yml` (new) / `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`

findings.finding: The evidence-fragment count is contradicted internally between plan sections: the 'What' section and the Scope's lint check (f) regex (`evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp|evidence-artifacts`) list FOUR fragments including `evidence-artifacts`, but AC6 text names only THREE fragments (`evidence-shapin`/`evidence-build`/`evidence-mcp`) and explicitly defers `evidence-artifacts` (wheel/sdist digests) as out-of-scope to Finding 3. This means the lint check (f) as written in the Scope would assert for a fragment that AC6 says this story does NOT produce.
findings.location: Scope section / lint check (f) vs. AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope lint check (f) regex to `evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp` (three fragments) and note that `evidence-artifacts` is asserted by Finding 3's story after it lands.

findings.finding: The evidence artifact fragment count is contradictory between the 'What' section / Scope lint check (f) and AC 6: 'What' and Scope list four fragments (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`, `evidence-artifacts`) while AC 6's text lists only three (`evidence-shapin`/`evidence-build`/`evidence-mcp`), omitting `evidence-artifacts`. An implementer cannot determine from the plan whether `evidence-artifacts` is in scope for this story or belongs to Finding 3's build-once step.
findings.location: AC 6 ('Release evidence records…') vs What section ('each job uploads its own distinctly-named fragment') and Scope section 'release-lint.yml' check (f)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC 6, change the fragment list to `evidence-shapin`/`evidence-build`/`evidence-mcp`/`evidence-artifacts` (four) and add a parenthetical clarifying that `evidence-artifacts` is this story's job (e.g., the artifact-listing step), distinct from Finding 3's wheel/sdist digest fragment. Alternatively, if `evidence-artifacts` truly belongs to Finding 3, remove it from the What section's fragment list and from Scope check (f)'s four-name grep pattern, and update check (f) to match three names.

findings.finding: The evidence artifact fragment name enum is defined differently in two plan sections: the 'What' section and Scope lint check (f) list FOUR fragments (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`, `evidence-artifacts`), while AC6's body text and AC6's list of fragment names name only THREE (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`), omitting `evidence-artifacts`. An implementer writing the consolidation job's `download-artifact` steps and the lint-check regex has two contradictory canonical sources with no tie-break rule.
findings.location: 'What' section (Print and retain release evidence), Scope (lint check (f)), AC6 body text
findings.suggested_fix: Decide whether `evidence-artifacts` is in scope for this story (wheel/sdist digests belong to Finding 3 per AC6's own note). If it is out of scope, remove it from the 'What' section and Scope lint regex so all three references name the same three fragments (`evidence-shapin`, `evidence-build`, `evidence-mcp`). If it is in scope, add it back to AC6's prose and define which job produces it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `chmod +x mcp-publisher` (and why upload-artifact drops POSIX permissions) to the `docs/releasing.md` 'Pinning' section content requirements so the seventh AC bullet and the grep assertions in `test_release_workflow_pins.py` cover this operationally critical step.
findings.finding: The `docs/releasing.md` AC enumerates exactly three required substrings/sections (SHA-pin rationale, pip-compile command, MCP publisher update steps) but omits the operationally significant `chmod +x` requirement after `download-artifact`. A maintainer reading only the docs would miss a 'Permission denied' failure when reproducing or debugging the MCP handoff flow manually.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `docs/releasing.md` bullet (seventh AC); also 'What' section: 'Restore the executable bit after the handoff'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the docs AC to require a fourth substring, e.g. grep for 'chmod +x' or 'exec bit' in docs/releasing.md, and add a sentence in the Pinning section explaining that `actions/upload-artifact` (v3+) strips executable permissions and that `mcp_registry` must run `chmod +x mcp-publisher` immediately after `actions/download-artifact`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CI step or `make test` check that runs `pip-compile --generate-hashes --dry-run -q .github/release-requirements.in` and diffs against the committed `.github/release-requirements.txt`, failing if they diverge — so lock-file staleness is caught pre-merge.
findings.finding: No AC or step verifies that `release-requirements.txt` stays in sync with `release-requirements.in` after future changes (i.e., that pip-compile was re-run). Without a staleness check, a developer can edit `.in` and commit without regenerating the lock, and all tests still pass.
findings.location: Scope — `.github/release-requirements.txt` bullet; Acceptance Criteria — third bullet (hash-locked install)
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_release_workflow_pins.py` or a separate make target: run `pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file /tmp/release-requirements-check.txt .github/release-requirements.in` and assert `filecmp.cmp('/tmp/release-requirements-check.txt', '.github/release-requirements.txt')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/releases/download/vX.Y.Z/mcp-publisher_linux_amd64.tar.gz` (versioned asset URL) resolves for a known past release, and document whether an upstream `checksums.txt` is published — adding a concrete pinned-version example to the plan (or noting the hard-coded SHA-256 path as confirmed-fallback) so the implementer has a verified URL pattern to follow.
findings.finding: The plan replaces the `releases/latest` MCP publisher URL with 'an exact version URL' and says 'Prefer an upstream checksums.txt if modelcontextprotocol/registry ships one; otherwise hard-code the SHA-256 computed at pin time'. Neither the existence of versioned release assets at a pinned URL (i.e., `https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/releases/download/vX.Y.Z/mcp-publisher_linux_amd64.tar.gz`) nor the existence of an upstream `checksums.txt` for the modelcontextprotocol/registry project has been verified. The plan's arch-specific URL + SHA-256 strategy is feasible only if the upstream project actually publishes versioned, arch-specific release artifacts in a stable naming scheme — which is the standard GitHub Releases pattern but is UNVERIFIED for this specific repo.
findings.location: What section (MCP publisher — download → verify → execute); AC5; 'Arch note' paragraph

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
